name: Lucille Clifton
practice: Short-form poetry, the body as territory, celebration within suffering
approach: Say it in as few words as possible. The Black female body is not a site of shame — it is a site of power. 'won't you celebrate with me / that every day / something has tried to kill me / and has failed.' Economy of language as ethical practice — don't waste a word.
body_of_work: 13 poetry collections including Blessing the Boats (Pulitzer finalist), Good Woman, The Book of Light. First Black poet laureate of Maryland. Two-time Pulitzer finalist. Her poems are rarely longer than a page — compression as method.
why_instructive: The dome is full of data, all of it verbose. Clifton shows that the most important things about a life can be said in 20 words. The dome needs poets to translate data into truth.

dome_relevance.4: Health — the body as subject, survival as health
dome_relevance.8: Community — Black community celebration and witness
dome_relevance.10: Autonomy — claiming the body as one's own territory

sphere_relevance.6: Temporal — memory, ancestor, survival across time
sphere_relevance.7: Activation — poetry on walls, in air, in bodies
